topic: My building's basement has a steel door labeled 1B but there is no apartment 1B in our building and I have a key that fits the lock
title: My Building's Basement Door Labeled 1B. No Such Apartment Exists. I Have the Key. | True Scary Story
description_intro: My apartment building's basement has a steel door labeled 1B. There is no apartment 1B in our building. The numbering on the floors runs 2A 2B 3A 3B all the way to 7B, nothing on the first floor or below. I have lived here for nine years. I noticed the door six months ago. I have a key that fits the lock. I am the only person in the building who can see the door.
description:
My apartment building's basement has a steel door labeled 1B. There is no apartment 1B in our building. The numbering on the floors runs 2A 2B 3A 3B all the way to 7B, nothing on the first floor or below. The basement is unfinished, used for storage and laundry. I have lived in this building for nine years. I noticed the door six months ago. I have a key that fits the lock. I am the only person in the building who can see the door.

The building is a six story brick walk up in central Pittsburgh, built in nineteen twenty four. The other tenants have lived here for in some cases forty years. None of them have ever heard of an apartment 1B. The building manager has shown me the original blueprints from his filing cabinet. There is no 1B on any of them. My key is the same brass key that came on my key ring when I signed the lease.

There is a recording behind the door. I have heard it through the steel. The recording describes my Wednesday mornings. Watch to the end. I am going to open the door tomorrow.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #apartmenthorror
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: My apartment building's basement has a steel door labeled 1B. There is no apartment 1B in our building. The numbering on the floors runs 2A 2B 3A 3B all the way up to 7B. Nothing on the first floor or below. The basement is unfinished, concrete walls and a single hanging bulb. The door is at the back, behind the laundry machines. It has a hand stenciled 1B on the steel in black paint. I noticed it six months ago. I went home and looked at my key ring. The brass key that came on my lease fits the lock. I have not turned it.

sections.narration: The building is a six story brick walk up in central Pittsburgh, built in nineteen twenty four. Twelve units, four to a floor, none on one. The first floor is the lobby, the management office, the mailroom, and a corridor to the back. The basement is reached through a steel door beside the elevator. The basement floor is concrete poured directly over the original wood, sloped toward a central drain. There are three coin operated washing machines, two dryers, and a long row of wire storage cages. Door 1B is past the cages, in the back left corner.

sections.narration: I have lived in this building for nine years. I do laundry every Wednesday morning at six. The basement is always empty at that hour. I am the early riser. I walk past the wire cages with my basket, load the washers, and sit on a folding chair near the dryers reading a book until the cycle finishes. I have done this for nine years. I had walked past door 1B four hundred and sixty seven times before I noticed it. The corner where it sits is partially behind a wooden storage shelf. The shelf was moved in May for an exterminator visit. The door has always been there.

sections.narration: I went up to the management office that morning and asked about it. The building manager is a man named Henrik who has been there since nineteen ninety six. He told me there had never been an apartment 1B. The numbering scheme starts at the second floor because the first floor is non residential. He pulled the original blueprints from his filing cabinet to show me. The basement was drawn with the laundry, the cages, and a small mechanical room. No 1B was on the plan. Henrik came down to look. He stood in the corner behind the cages. He could not see the door. He told me the wall was solid steel.
image_prompts: wide interior of a small building management office with a wooden desk and a filing cabinet, a man seen from behind in a corduroy jacket, no face visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I assumed Henrik was mistaken. I had seen the door six times by then. I went home, drew the basement layout on a sheet of paper, and tried to convince myself I was wrong about where the door was. I marked the cages, the washers, the dryers, the mechanical room. I marked the corner where the door stood. Henrik had been right about the basement layout in every other detail. The door was simply not in his version. I thought I was misremembering. I went down at six the next morning. The door was there. I touched the painted 1B on the steel. The paint was old.

sections.narration: I asked the other tenants over the following weeks. Mrs Bellinger on three has lived here for forty one years. She did not remember a 1B. Mr Stevens on five has been here since nineteen eighty eight. He did not remember it either. He came down with me to look. He stood beside me in the corner. He saw a wall. He could not see the door. I touched the door in front of him. He saw my hand pressed against an empty section of basement wall. Henrik came down again. He saw a wall too. I am the only person in the building who can see this door.

sections.narration: I started carrying a small recording app on my phone when I went to the laundry. I left it running while the washers ran. The first recording captured nothing but the machines. The second captured what sounded like a chair being scraped across a wood floor. I played it back four times to be sure. There were no wood floors anywhere in the basement. The third recording, made on a Wednesday at six twenty AM, picked up a voice. The voice was muffled. Coming from beyond the door. It was speaking in measured sentences, the way you read aloud. I could not make out the words. The recording is forty seconds long.

sections.narration: I started bringing the phone closer to the door. Each Wednesday morning the recording captured more. The chair, the voice, eventually a faint music underneath the voice, jazz from a small speaker. The voice was reading. I sent the audio to a friend who works in sound design. She cleaned it up and sent it back. The voice was reading a journal entry. The entry was dated two thousand sixteen. It described a Wednesday morning. It described a woman doing her laundry in the basement of her building. She had been standing at the dryers for forty minutes. She did not notice the door behind her. The entry ended there.

sections.narration: The most recent recording captured a different voice. A man's voice, calmer, also reading. The entry was dated two thousand twenty four. It described a Wednesday morning. It described a woman doing her laundry in the basement. She had stood at the dryers for forty minutes. She had not yet tried the key. The entry stopped there. I did not move. I stood at the door for an hour. I held the brass key in my hand. I felt the door breathe through the steel. It was warmer than the surrounding wall. I went upstairs without trying the key. The recording continued for nine more minutes after I left.

sections.narration: I have not gone to the basement in three weeks. I do laundry at the dry cleaners up the block now. They charge by the pound. Mrs Bellinger asked me why I stopped. I said the basement was cold. She nodded. She said it had always been cold. The brass key is still on my key ring. I cannot take it off. The split ring is bent in a way that holds it in place. I cannot pry it loose. The key has started to feel warm in my pocket near the building. I am going down tomorrow at six. I want to know what is on the other side.
